text:
Barbell Row (Bent-Over Row) — Setup and Mechanics

Starting position: Bar on the floor or set in a rack at mid-shin height. Stance hip-width, toes slightly out. Hinge forward until the torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or 45 degrees for a more upright variant — both are valid). The back is neutral — not rounded, not hyperextended. The bar hangs at arm's length directly below the shoulder.

Torso angle: Parallel to the floor (Pendlay row variant) creates a stricter pull and eliminates momentum. 45-degree torso angle allows slightly more load and is the most common form seen in powerlifting accessory work. Both are legitimate — choose based on training goal.

Grip: Slightly wider than shoulder-width, pronated (overhand). Some lifters use a supinated (underhand) grip which shifts emphasis slightly toward the biceps and lower lat. Grip width affects the angle of pull and therefore which portion of the back is most heavily recruited.

Pull path: Bar drives toward the lower abdomen or navel — not toward the chest (that's an upright row) and not toward the hip (that's too low). The elbows travel back and slightly out, at roughly 45 degrees from the torso. At the top, the elbows should be at or slightly above the torso level — full scapular retraction with the bar touching the body.

Leg drive and cheating: A small amount of lower body drive (body English) is acceptable for overloading, but the majority of the movement should be arm and back. Excessive heaving that converts the movement to a near-deadlift each rep defeats the purpose of a rowing exercise.

Barbell Row — Muscles Worked and Common Errors

Primary movers: Latissimus dorsi (shoulder extension, adduction), middle and lower trapezius (scapular retraction and depression), rhomboids (retraction), posterior deltoid, biceps brachii and brachialis (elbow flexion).

Secondary: Erector spinae (isometric stabilizer maintaining hinge position), teres major, infraspinatus.

Scapular mechanics: The row is not just an arm movement — full range of motion requires active scapular retraction at the top of the pull. Many lifters let the scapulae remain protracted throughout, which reduces the stimulus to the middle back and puts the shoulder in an impingement-prone position. Cue: 'lead with the elbows, then pinch the shoulder blades at the top.'

Common errors:
1. Jerking with the lower back — the torso rises and falls with each rep, converting the movement to a partial deadlift. Fix: maintain a fixed hip hinge position, brace the core, keep the lower back still.
2. Bar touching the chest instead of the abdomen — indicates grip too wide or elbows flaring too much; primarily trains the rear delts and upper back rather than the lats. Fix: pull toward the navel, elbows at 45 degrees.
3. No scapular retraction at top — reduces middle back stimulus. Fix: pause 1 second at the top and consciously squeeze the shoulder blades.
4. Partial range of motion — stopping short before the bar touches the body reduces the stimulus at peak contraction. Fix: full range, full contact.
5. Rounded lower back — high-risk under load. Fix: reduce load, re-establish hip hinge mechanics.
